topic: Someone receives an invitation to their high school reunion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The embossed card felt heavy in Amelia’s hand, the cream paper cool against her fingertips. A high school reunion. The invitation, crisp and formal, was addressed to her, a stark reminder of the years she’d spent trying to disappear. Her stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath her ribs. She traced the elegant script of the sender’s name—a name she vaguely remembered, a ghost from a distant past. Looking at the date, the sheer expanse of time felt like a physical weight pressing down on her shoulders. She tucked the invitation into the deepest pocket of her jeans, suddenly desperate for the anonymity of the fabric. The thought of navigating those familiar hallways, of facing faces she hadn't seen in decades, made her palms slick with sudden perspiration.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the invitation, his gaze flickering over the familiar crest of the school logo. A slow, hot flush crept up his neck, staining his cheeks a deep crimson. He fiddled incessantly with the frayed edge of the paper, his knuckles turning white. Every muscle in his jaw felt tight, resisting the urge to simply crumple the thing and throw it away. He pictured the gathering—the forced laughter, the well-rehearsed anecdotes. The silence of his apartment amplified the frantic drumming in his ears. *They’re all doing well,* a small, bitter voice whispered in his mind, immediately followed by a sharp intake of breath. He quickly shoved the card into a drawer, the sound of the latch clicking sounding deafeningly loud in the quiet room.

[story 3]

When Sarah received the mail, she didn't look at the contents immediately. She held the envelope near the window, letting the afternoon sun illuminate the faded ink. A strange tremor ran through her hands, making the paper tremble slightly. The invitation felt like an accusation, a spotlight suddenly focused on every misstep she’d ever taken. She reread the line specifying the event date, and a wave of nausea washed over her. She dropped the card onto the wooden table with a soft thud, immediately crossing her arms tightly across her chest, trying to physically shrink into the chair. She focused intensely on the grain of the wood, willing herself to become invisible, to dissolve into the background noise of the room.
